Jesus focused more on preaching, which was a better use of his time, than on baptism. He honored his disciples by letting them baptize people. He taught that the benefit of sacraments depends not on who performs them.

There was intense animosity between Samaritans and Jews. Jesus’s route from Judea to Galilee passed through Samaria. We should avoid places of temptation unless absolutely necessary, and then we should pass through quickly. Here, we see our Lord Jesus experiencing the normal fatigue of travelers. This shows that he was truly a man. Toil came with sin; therefore, Christ, having become a curse for us, submitted to it. He was also poor, traveling everywhere on foot. Being tired, he sat by the well; he had no place to rest. He sat as weary travelers do. Surely, we should readily submit to being like the Son of God in such things. Jesus asked a woman for water. She was surprised that he did not show anger toward Samaritans, which was common among his own people. Moderate people on all sides are often misunderstood. Jesus used this opportunity to teach her about spiritual truths; he converted her by showing her ignorance and need of a Savior. By “living water” is meant the Spirit. This comparison had been promised in the Old Testament as part of the blessing of the Messiah. The graces of the Spirit, and his comforts, satisfy the thirsty soul that knows its own nature and needs. What Jesus said figuratively, she took literally. Jesus pointed out that the water from Jacob’s well provided only a temporary satisfaction. Whoever drinks from any source of comfort will thirst again. But whoever partakes of the Spirit of grace, and the comforts of the gospel, will never lack what abundantly satisfies their soul. People with carnal hearts look for only earthly things. “Give it to me,” she said, “not that I may have everlasting life,” which Jesus offered, “but so that I do not need to come here to draw water.” The carnal mind is very skilled at avoiding convictions and preventing them from taking hold. But how closely our Lord Jesus brought the conviction to her conscience! He severely addressed her present way of living. The woman acknowledged Jesus as a prophet. The power of his word to search the heart, and convict the conscience of hidden things, proves his divine authority. We should be less concerned with arguments when we realize they are temporary. God will remain the same—our Father; but all disagreements about where to worship will end. Reason tells us to consider decency and convenience in places of worship; but religion does not give preference to one place over another in terms of holiness or approval with God. The Jews were certainly correct. Those who have learned something about God through Scripture know whom they worship. The word of salvation came from the Jews, and it spread to other nations through them. Jesus preferred Jewish worship to Samaritan worship, yet here he spoke of the former as soon to be abolished. God was about to reveal himself as the Father of all believers in every nation. The human spirit, influenced by the Holy Spirit, must worship God and have fellowship with him. Spiritual feelings, such as fervent prayers, requests, and thanksgivings, are the worship of an upright heart, where God finds delight and glory. The woman wanted to postpone the matter until the Messiah arrived. But Jesus said, “I am the one speaking to you.” She was a foreign and hostile Samaritan; simply speaking with her was considered shameful by some. Yet, to this woman, Jesus revealed himself more fully than he had to any of his disciples. No past sins can prevent us from being accepted if we humble ourselves before him, believing in him as the Christ, the Savior of the world.

The disciples were surprised that Jesus spoke with a Samaritan. But they knew it was for a good reason and purpose. Therefore, when unexpected difficulties arise in God’s word or providence, we should be confident that what Jesus says and does is right. Two things affected the woman: his knowledge and the power of his words. Jesus knows all the thoughts, words, and actions of everyone. He revealed her secret sins with power. She focused on that part of Jesus's teaching, which many would have been hesitant to repeat; but the knowledge of Christ, into which we are led by conviction of sin, is most likely to be true and saving. They came to him: those who want to know Christ must meet him where he reveals himself. Our Master has given us an example so that we may learn to do God’s will as he did—with diligence, as if it were a business; with delight and pleasure in it. Jesus compared his work to harvest work. The gospel is like a harvest that is appointed and anticipated before it arrives. Harvest time is busy time; everyone must be at work then. Harvest time is short, and the work must be done then or not at all; similarly, the time of the gospel is a season which, if missed, cannot be repeated. God sometimes uses weak and unlikely people to begin and carry out good works. Jesus, by teaching one poor woman, spread knowledge throughout an entire town. Blessed are those who are not offended by Christ. Those taught by God truly desire to learn more. It adds much to our love for Christ and his word if it overcomes prejudices. Their faith grew: in what they believed—that he is the Savior of the world; in their certainty—that this is indeed the Christ; and in the basis of their belief—because they had heard him themselves.

The man was a nobleman, yet his son was sick. Honors and titles offer no protection from sickness or death. Even great men must go to God, becoming beggars. The nobleman did not stop asking until he received what he wanted. But at first, he showed a lack of faith in the power of Christ. It is difficult to believe that distance in time and place cannot hinder our knowledge of, or access to, God’s mercy and power. Jesus gave an answer of peace. Jesus's statement that the soul lives brings life. The father went home, showing the sincerity of his faith. His servants met him with news of his son’s recovery. Good news will come to those who hope in God’s word. Carefully comparing Jesus’s works with his word confirms our faith. And the healing brought salvation to the whole family. Thus, an experience of the power of one word from Christ can establish Christ's authority in a person’s soul. The entire family believed as well. They grew to love Jesus because of the miracle. Knowledge of Christ continues to spread through families, bringing health and salvation to their souls.
